coredata.dc:title: Preparation and characterization of chiral carbon dots with red emission
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Hao
Chen, Yonglin
Liang, Zhi-Bin
Shen, Anze
Chen, Lintao
Guo, Jiazhuang
Wang, Cai-Feng
Chen, Su
coredata.dc:description: Methods for the synthesis of chiral carbon dots (CDs) possessing bright long-wavelength emission are highly desirable. Here, we develop red-emissive chiral CDs from hydrothermal treatment of L-/D-lysine and o-phenylenediamine (OPD). Pure chiral CDs are obtained by filtration and dialysis. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra, photoluminescence (PL) spectra, PL quantum yield (QY), and PL decay of the chiral CDs are thoroughly studied. The chiral CDs exhibit bright red fluorescence with PL QY in the range of 26.6%–42.2% in different solvents. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) indicate that the chiral CDs have a mean particle size of 2.7 nm, and consist of graphite-like carbons and abundant functional groups at their surfaces. Circular dichroism spectroscopy shows new Cotton bands at about 300 and 610 nm for chiral CDs differed from those of L-/D-lysine, revealing the generation of optical chirality for CDs. Then transparent optical resins made of CDs/epoxy resin composites are prepared, possessing red fluorescence and circular dichroism, broadening potential applications of solid materials with chiroptical properties. Finally, we discuss the possible origins of chirality and fluorescence of the CDs. The exploitation of highly red-emissive chiral CDs may promote the development and application of chiral CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Chirality
Fluorescence
Synthesis
Circular dichroism
Red emission

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of red-emissive chiral CDs/epoxy resin composites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation procedure of red-emissive L-/D-CDs through hydrothermal method. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

PL intensity and PL QYs of L-CDs in aqueous solution prepared with (a) different molar ratios of L-lysine to OPD and (b) different reaction times (λex = 560 nm). (c) UV–vis absorption spectrum and PL spectra under excitation wavelengths ranging from 500 to 580 nm for L-CDs in aqueous solution. (d) PL decay of L-CDs in aqueous solution, collected at 625 nm (λex = 365 nm). (e) Photographs (taken under UV light) and PL spectra of L-CDs in aqueous solution, ethanol, DMF, and DMSO, respectively. (f) PL QYs (red line) and optimal emission wavelengths (black line) of L-CDs in different solvents. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Structural characterizations of L-CDs. (a) TEM image. Inset: particle size distribution of L-CDs. (b) High-resolution TEM image. The inset represents the FFT and inverse FFT pattern of a single L-CD. (c) XRD pattern and (d) FT-IR spectrum of L-CDs. (e) XPS survey, and high-resolution XPS (f) C 1 s, (g) N 1 s, and (h) O 1 s spectra of L-CDs.

Circular dichroism spectra of (a) L-lysine and D-lysine, and (b) L-CDs and D-CDs. (c) UV − vis absorption and PL emission spectra of L-CDs/epoxy resin composites. Inset: photographs of the corresponding optical resin under daylight and 365 nm UV light. (d) Circular dichroism spectra of L-CDs/epoxy resin and D-CDs/epoxy resin composites.
A possible formation mechanism for L-/D-CDs.

head.ce:title: Preparation and characterization of chiral carbon dots with red emission

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering, College of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, 5 Xin Mofan Road, Nanjing 210009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering, College of Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering, College of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, 5 Xin Mofan Road, Nanjing 210009, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Methods for the synthesis of chiral carbon dots (CDs) possessing bright long-wavelength emission are highly desirable. Here, we develop red-emissive chiral CDs from hydrothermal treatment of L-/D-lysine and o-phenylenediamine (OPD). Pure chiral CDs are obtained by filtration and dialysis. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra, photoluminescence (PL) spectra, PL quantum yield (QY), and PL decay of the chiral CDs are thoroughly studied. The chiral CDs exhibit bright red fluorescence with PL QY in the range of 26.6%–42.2% in different solvents. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) indicate that the chiral CDs have a mean particle size of 2.7 nm, and consist of graphite-like carbons and abundant functional groups at their surfaces. Circular dichroism spectroscopy shows new Cotton bands at about 300 and 610 nm for chiral CDs differed from those of L-/D-lysine, revealing the generation of optical chirality for CDs. Then transparent optical resins made of CDs/epoxy resin composites are prepared, possessing red fluorescence and circular dichroism, broadening potential applications of solid materials with chiroptical properties. Finally, we discuss the possible origins of chirality and fluorescence of the CDs. The exploitation of highly red-emissive chiral CDs may promote the development and application of chiral CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chiral artificial materials, exhibiting interesting optical properties such as circular dichroism [1], circularly polarized luminescence [2], or nonlinear optics [3], have received considerable attention. Till now, chiral organic molecules [4], chiral metal nanoparticles [5], and chiral semiconductor quantum dots [6] have been extensively developed with excellent optical properties [7–9]. Particularly, carbon dots (CDs) are a unique type of carbon-based nanomaterials that exhibit numerous unique advantages, including excellent photoluminescence (PL) [10,11], good biocompatibility [12,13], facile synthesis process [14,15], low toxicity [16], and high solubility in water and most organic solvents [17]. These fascinating features make them attractive as promising substitutes to heavy metal-based semiconductor quantum dots in various applications [18]. Moreover, the introduction of chirality into CDs effectively broadens their optical characteristics like chiroptical properties, as well as the range of applications such as chiral recognition [19], asymmetric catalysis [20], or biomedicine [21].

While much of this research has been directed toward chiral CDs with short-wavelength emission [22,23], some success has also been achieved in long-wavelength-emissive chiral CDs. For instance, in 2016, Suzuki et al. synthesized chiral CDs exhibiting green emission at 530 nm through covalently attaching L-/D-cysteine to the surface functional groups of CDs [24]. Di Noja et al. prepared chiral CDs using citric acid, binaphthyl diamines, and 1,4-benzoquinone as precursors [25], to show PL wavelength of 480 nm and quantum yield (QY) of 0.4%, as well as circularly polarized luminescence at around 500 nm. In 2018, Li et al. hydrothermally treated L-/D-cysteine to prepare green-emissive chiral CDs with PL QY of 41.26% [26]. In 2021, Ru et al. synthesized blue-, yellow-, and red-emissive chiral CDs with PL QYs between 31%–54% using o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and L-/D-tryptophan as precursors by solvothermal method [27]. In 2023, Wei et al. fabricated red-emissive chiral CDs with PL QY of 6.80% with use of citric acid, urea, and L-/D-isoleucine as precursors [19]. It is worth noting that despite encouraging advancements, chiral CDs with bright long-wavelength especially red emission are still rare, and the development of long-wavelength-emissive chiral CDs with relatively high PL QY will greatly facilitate their applicability to various application fields like optoelectronics, biology and medicine.

In this study, red-emissive chiral CDs were developed from hydrothermal treatment of OPD and L-/D-lysine. And then a dialysis experiment was carried out to obtain pure chiral CDs. Optical characterizations indicated that the chiral CDs exhibit bright red fluorescence with PL QYs in the range of 26.6%–42.2% in different solvents. Structural characterizations suggested that chiral CDs are nanoparticles composed of graphite-like carbons with abundant surface functional groups. Furthermore, circular dichroism spectroscopy was performed to study chiroptical properties of red-emissive chiral CDs. Then chiral CDs/epoxy resin composites with circular dichroism and red emission were prepared, to provide transparent optical resins. Also proposed were the possible formation mechanisms of red-emissive chiral CDs. This work may be beneficial for the development and application of long-wavelength-emitting chiral CDs.

L-lysine (98%), D-lysine (98%), L-alanine (98%), o-phenylenediamine (OPD, 99.5%), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.5%) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, 99.5%) were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. L-valine (98%) and L-cysteine (99%) were obtained from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. L-proline (99.5%) was obtained from Shanghai Maclean's Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Anhydrous ethanol, concentrated sulfuric acid (98%) and deionized water were obtained from Shanghai Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Phenolic epoxy resin E51 and epoxy resin curing agent H0615 were purchased from Shenzhen Jitian Chemical Co., Ltd.

The synthesis of red-emissive chiral CDs was performed through a one-pot hydrothermal treatment of OPD and various chiral amino acids in diluted sulfuric acid (5 wt%). Typically, 1 mmol OPD and 1 mmol L-/D-lysine were dissolved in 15 mL diluted sulfuric acid (5 wt%) and then transferred into a poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave. Subsequently, the autoclave was put into an oven and heated at 180 °C for 10 h, and then cooled to room temperature. A red fluorescent suspension was collected from the autoclave, filtered using a filtration membrane with 0.22 μm millipores to exclude large particle impurities. Then the filtrate was dialyzed against deionized water for 3 days (MWCO = 3500 Da, changing water twice a day) to remove unreacted precursors, sulfuric acid, and other small molecules, followed by freeze drying for 5 days to obtain pure chiral CDs powder for further characterizations. The preparation of other chiral CDs was carried out in a similar way as described above, except for replacing the lysine with other amino acids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of red-emissive chiral CDs/epoxy resin composites
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, chiral CDs (1 mg) were dissolved in DMF (1 mL), and added into epoxy resin (4 g). Then curing agent (1 g) was put into the mixture. Red-emissive chiral CDs/epoxy resin composites were obtained after 24 h curing at room temperature.
A desired amount of red emissive chiral CDs/epoxy resin before curing, prepared as described in Section 2.3, was put on a 365 nm UV LED chip, and then left at room temperature for 24 h curing.

Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of chiral CDs were recorded by a UV-3600 spectrometer. Excitation spectra and PL emission spectra of chiral CDs were performed on an Edinburgh FLS 980 spectrometer with a PMT980 detector, on which an integrating sphere was further fitted to obtain their absolute PL QY, and PL decay data of chiral CDs were obtained utilizing a time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) methodology. Unless specifically mentioned, diluted solutions of chiral CDs were used for the above optical characterizations, and the solution sample for absolute PL QY measurement was diluted to behave ∼30% absorption of excitation light. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) of chiral CDs was studied on a FEI Tecnai G2 F20 electron microscope. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried out using a Bruker AXS D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was performed with a Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of chiral CDs was determined with a Scientific K-Alpha XPS system. Circular dichroism spectroscopy for chiral CDs was provided by JASCO J-1500 spectrophotometer.

In this paper, chiral CDs with bright long-wavelength emission could be prepared by hydrothermal treatment of OPD with diverse chiral amino acids. As shown in Fig. 1 , red-emissive chiral CDs, L-CDs and D-CDs, could be synthesized from hydrothermal treatment of OPD with L-lysine and D-lysine in diluted sulfuric acid (5 wt%) at 180 °C for 10 h, respectively. Further purification via filtration and dialysis, followed by freeze drying, afforded pure powder products of chiral CDs (Fig. S1), which show aggregation-caused fluorescence quenching [28]. These powder products of chiral CDs could be well re-dispersed into aqueous solution (5 wt% sulfuric acid) and most organic solvents such as ethanol, DMF, and DMSO, to exhibit bright red fluorescence. The syntheses, structural characterizations, and optical properties of these red-emissive chiral CDs and their integration into optical resins were then thoroughly investigated.

To obtain chiral CDs with good fluorescence, the selection of proper experimental reaction variables was performed, involving the molar ratio of L-lysine to OPD, the reaction time, and even the species of chiral amino acids. And the absolute PL QY measurements were carried out for the products using an integrating sphere following previously reported testing methods [29,30]. To investigate the optimal proportion of precursors, five sets of L-CDs were synthesized with different molar ratios of L-lysine to OPD varying from 1:0.3 to 1:0.5, 1:1, 1:2, 1:3 by fixing reaction temperature and time at 180 °C and 10 h, respectively (Fig. 2a). The results indicate that the PL QY of the CDs prepared with a L-lysine to OPD molar ratio of 1:1 could reach a maximum of 26%. Control experiments without use of either OPD or L-lysine were also conducted. Control experiment using L-lysine as the sole precursor provided the products exhibiting only blue fluorescence (Fig. S2). Whereas, the products obtained using OPD as the precursor display red fluorescence similar to that of L-CDs but with lower PL QY (17%) (Fig. S3). These results suggest that OPD is crucial for the formation of red emissive CDs, while the reaction of OPD with L-CDs could yield red emissive CDs with favorable PL QY and chirality. Then the effect of reaction time (6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16 h) on the PL QY of CDs was researched, showing that CDs with optimal fluorescence could be obtained at a reaction time of 10 h (Fig. 2b). Moreover, by using different chiral amino acids, such as L-proline, L-valine, L-alanine, or L-cysteine, to react with OPD under the same reaction conditions described above, different red-emissive chiral CDs were prepared (Fig. S4). Their optimal PL emission wavelengths are all located at about 625 nm with PL QY values between 15% and 22%. The results suggest that L-lysine is a better choice than other amino acids to prepare relatively bright red-emissive chiral CDs. Thus, the reaction conditions used for the preparation of L-/D-CDs are determined to be 180 °C for 10 h with an L-/D-lysine to the OPD molar ratio of 1:1.

Fig. 2c represents the optical properties of the aqueous solution of L-CDs prepared under optimized reaction conditions. The UV–vis absorption peak at 292 nm may be ascribed to π-π* transition of CC bonds, while the absorption bands between 542 and 653 nm with two peaks located at 568 nm and 617 nm indicate that these samples possess different surface states [31,32]. Accordingly, the excitation spectrum of L-CDs shows excitation bands around 564 and 610 nm, matching well with their absorption bands (Fig. S5). The PL spectra of L-CDs show excitation-independent PL emission peak position. With the increase in the excitation wavelength from 500 to 580 nm, the PL emission wavelength is fixed at 625 nm, along with a shoulder at 675 nm. The optimal emission with a narrow FWHM of 37 nm was observed under excitation wavelength of 560 nm. To understand fluorescence dynamics of L-CDs, the time-resolved PL decay was measured (Fig. 2d). The decay curve could be well fitted with a dual-exponential function Y(t):
Y
t
=
B 1
e
− t /
τ 1
+
B 2
e
− t /
τ 2
wherein B1 and B2 represent fractional contributions of lifetimes τ1 and τ2, respectively. The average lifetime ͞τ of PL decay was determined by the following expression:
τ ¯
=
B 1
τ 1 2
+
B 2
τ 2 2
B 1
τ 1
+
B 2
τ 2

The result indicates a dual-exponential decay process of L-CDs with an average lifetime ͞τ of 3.60 ns, which may involve complicated recombination processes for carbonized cores with rich surface states [32–36].

Another indication is that the PL properties of chiral CDs change in different solvents. With the excitation of 365 nm UV light, L-CDs can emit bright red fluorescence and different PL spectra in aqueous solution, ethanol, DMF, and DMSO (Fig. 2e). The PL spectra vary dramatically with the different type of solvent, indicating the surface state related emissions for L-CDs. The most common one is the solute–solvents interaction, which are highly sensitive to their surrounding environment [37]. Changes in solvent polarity can significantly impact the energy gap between the ground and excited states, as well as the magnitude of the transition dipole moment, thereby causing variations in the fluorescence spectrum, emission wavelength, and PL QY [36–38]. Specifically, the acidic aqueous solution of L-CDs has dual emission centered at 625 and 675 nm, the ethanol solution of L-CDs exhibits three emission peaks located at 600, 650, and 715 nm, the DMF solution of L-CDs shows two PL peaks at 590 and 650 nm, and the DMSO solution of L-CDs displays two PL peaks at 600 and 650 nm. Overall, the PL QYs of L-CDs in aqueous solution, ethanol, DMF, and DMSO are 26.6%, 29.7%, 41.6%, and 42.2%, respectively (Fig. 2f, Fig. S6), which are very high values among the previous works about red-emission chiral CDs [19,39].

The formation of L-CDs was verified using TEM (Fig. 3a). The CDs are uniformly dispersed without any aggregation. Statistical analysis and Gaussian fitting of particle size distribution data indicate that the average particle size of L-CDs is 2.7 nm. High-resolution TEM image, fast Fourier transform (FFT), and inverse FFT pattern results of L-CDs are shown in Fig. 3b, indicating that L-CDs have crystalline structure and obvious lattice stripes with a stripe spacing of 0.21 nm, assigned to the d-spacing of graphene (100). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of L-CDs displays an evident peak centered at around 2θ = 23o (Fig. 3c), ascribed to the (002) plane of the graphite structure. These results indicate that L-CDs possess a high extent of graphitization.

Then FT-IR and XPS spectra of the CDs were characterized to determine their surface structure. FT-IR spectrum shows an absorption at 3415 cm−1, attributed to the OH and/or NH stretching vibrations. Peaks at 2915, 1625, 1410, and 1112 cm−1 should be assigned to CH, CO, CN, and CO stretching vibrations, respectively (Fig. 3d). The distinct absorption peaks located at 1530 cm−1 could be ascribed to NH bending vibration. In addition, the vibration intensities of CN at 1585 cm−1 and CC at 1472 cm−1 indicate the presence of graphitic N and graphitic C. XPS survey displays three peaks at 285 eV, 400 eV, and 532 eV, which implies the CDs mainly include the chemical elements of C, N, and O (corresponding atomic content: 52.0%, 8.6%, 39.4%), respectively (Fig. 3e). The high-resolution XPS C 1 s shows signals for C-C/C=C (284.5 eV), CN (285 eV), CO (286 eV), and CO (288.6 eV) (Fig. 3f). N 1 s spectra reveal that N exists in the forms of pyridinic N (399.1 eV), pyrrolic N (399.9 eV), and graphitic N (401.3 eV) (Fig. 3g). High-resolution O 1 s spectra are composed of two signals at 531.6 and 532.4 eV, suggesting the existence of CO and CO bonds (Fig. 3h). These results correspond well with the FT-IR characterization, which suggest there are abundant surface groups, including hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl, and amino groups, grafted on graphite-like carbon core of L-CDs.

To investigate the chirality of synthesized L-/D-CDs, circular dichroism spectroscopy was employed to characterize L-CDs and D-CDs. As presented in Fig. 4a, L-lysine and D-lysine exhibit symmetrical circular dichroism signals around 220 nm, and L-lysine shows a positive Cotton effect while D-lysine has a negative signal. Whereas, the aqueous solutions of L-CDs and D-CDs display new circular dichroism signals at about 300 and 610 nm (Fig. 4b), corresponding to absorption peaks of their UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 2c), which are quite different from those of chiral precursor. These results imply that L-/D-lysine reacts with OPD and undergoes processes including polymerization and carbonization to ultimately form chiral CDs, enabling the generation of optical chirality for CDs and the consequent appearance of new circular dichroism signals [40]. These new circular dichroism signals above 300 nm are probablly originated from the n − π* transition of the surface state of chiral CDs [41,42], but not from wreckage of the precursor L-/D-lysine which displays circular dichroism signal around 220 nm. Table S1 summarizes the optical properties and circular dichroisms of chiral CDs developed in this work and in literatures. We can see that most of previously reported chiral CDs show short-wavelength emission, and this work represents examples of chiral CDs exhibiting long-wavelength emission with favorable PL QY and chiroptical properties.

In addition, transparent optical resins made of L-CDs/epoxy resin and D-CDs/epoxy resin composites were prepared, respectively, to exhibit both fluorescence and chiroptical properties. The optical properties of L-CDs/epoxy resin composite were investigated as an example (Fig. 4c, Fig. S7). L-CDs/epoxy resin composite displays two adjacent absorption peaks at 526 and 557 nm, while the emission spectra exhibit a slight excitation dependence with an optimum excitation and emission wavelengths located at 590 and 650 nm, respectively (Fig. 4c, Fig. S7a). The red-shift PL emission from the DMF solution to the composite may be ascribed to reabsorption, and fluorescence resonance energy transfer [43,44], inner filter effect [45]. The time-resolved PL decay spectrum demonstrates a dual-exponential decay process of the L-CDs/epoxy resin composite with an average lifetime of 5.69 ns (Fig. S7b). The chiroptical properties of L-CDs/epoxy resin and D-CDs/epoxy resin composites were investigated by circular dichroism spectroscopy, which display negative/positive Cotton effect at 570 nm, respectively. These optical resins with desirable chiroptical properties might be useful for various optoelectronic applications. As an example, a red-light LED lamp was constructed with use of chiral CDs/epoxy resin composites as colour-converting materials (Fig. S8). Continuous effort is required for chiroptical application of red-emissive chiral CDs and their composites in future work.

Overall, the above optical characterizations demonstrate that the red-emissive chiral CDs have solvent-dependent and excitation-independent optical properties with relatively high PL QY, which are generally dominated by surface states of CDs [30,46,47]. The carbon core states typically exhibit excitation-dependent optical properties with lower PL QY [48]. Structural characterizations confirm the composition of graphite-like carbon cores with abundant surface functional groups. Circular dichroism spectroscopy further verifies the contribution of surface state of chiral CDs [41,42], as well as the generation of optical chirality for CDs. Based on the above results and previous research [31,35,36,46,49], we propose a mechanism for the formation, fluorescence, and chirality of L-/D-CDs (Fig. 5 ). The early forms of CDs, planar structures, cross-linked molecules, are generated through hydrothermal treatment of OPD and L-/D-lysine under acidic condition, allowing the dehydration, carbonization, longitudinal and lateral growth process of precursors to occur [43]. Small molecular fluorophores with structure analogous to protonated 2,3-diaminophe-nazine (P-2.3-DAPN) [46], 5,14-dihydroquinoxalino[2,3-b] phenazine (DHQP) [35], quinoxalino[2,3-b]phenazine-2,3-diamine (QXPDA) [31] and some others unknown are also generated in this progress. L-/D-CDs could be formed in the mixtures by further reaction, carbonization or self-assembled of the planar structures. Then L-/D-CDs functionalized with surface fluorophores as well as chirality are obtained, further purified via filtration and dialysis treatment of the mixture to remove all the impurities of small molecules. Therefore, L-/D-CDs show strong red fluorescence and possess circular dichroism signals. The instructive possible formation, fluorescence, and chirality mechanism for L-/D-CDs might be helpful for synthesizing other CDs with chiroptical properties.

In summary, the method for preparation of red-emissive chiral CDs through hydrothermal treatment of L-/D-lysine and OPD in an acidic aqueous solution was realized. Pure powder products of L-/D-CDs were obtained by filtration, dialysis and freeze drying. The optical properties of L-/D-CDs were generally studied via UV–vis absorption, PL, PL QY, and PL decay. And the chiral CDs could be redissolved in aqueous solution, ethanol, DMF, and DMSO, to exhibit bright red fluorescence with optimal emission wavelength varied from 590 to 625 nm and PL QYs of 26.6%, 29.7%, 41.6%, and 42.2%, respectively. TEM, XRD, FTIR, and XPS indicated that chiral CDs consist of graphite-like carbon core and abundant surface functional groups. Moreover, Circular dichroism spectroscopy shows that L-/D-CDs possessed interesting chiroptical properties, to exhibit circular dichroism signals differed from those of L-/D-lysine, revealing the successful generation of optical chirality in CDs. Then transparent optical resins with both red fluorescence and circular dichroism were further fabricated. Based on these results and previous research, we considered that L-/D-CDs might be bonded with red molecular fluorophores as well as chiral carbon atom groups derived from L-/D-lysine, to produce red PL emission and circular dichroism signals. This work provides a facile approach to prepare chiral CDs with long-wavelength emission and interesting chiroptical properties, which might promote the development and application of chiral CDs.

Hao Chen: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yonglin Chen: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Formal analysis. Zhi-Bin Liang: Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Anze Shen: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Lintao Chen: Investigation, Formal analysis. Jiazhuang Guo: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Cai-Feng Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Su Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Key Research and Development Program of China (2022YFC2104600), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21978132 and 21736006), Postdoctoral Fellowship Program of CPSF (GZC20231112), and Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matchar.2024.114026.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped chiral carbon quantum dots with independent photoluminescence and chirality

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Hu, Y. Sun, Y. Zhou, L. Bai, Y. Zhang, M. Han, H. Huang, Y. Liu, Z. Kang, Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped chiral carbon quantum dots with independent photoluminescence and chirality, Inorg. Chem. Front. 4 (2017) 946-953.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Frontiers in circularly polarized luminescence: molecular design, self-assembly, nanomaterials, and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.-L. Gong, X. Zhu, Z. Zhou, S.-W. Zhang, D. Yang, B. Zhao, Y.-P. Zhang, J. Deng, Y. Cheng, Y.-X. Zheng, S.-Q. Zang, H. Kuang, P. dDuan, M. Yuan, C.-F. Chen, Y.S. Zhao, Y.-W. Zhong, B.Z. Tang, M. Liu, Frontiers in circularly polarized luminescence: molecular design, self-assembly, nanomaterials, and applications, Sci. Chi. Chem. 64 (2021) 2060-2104.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chiral supramolecular gold-cysteine nanoparticles: Chiroptical and nonlinear optical properties

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I. Russier-Antoine, F. Bertorelle, A. Kulesza, A. Soleilhac, A. Bensalah-Ledoux, S. Guy, P. Dugourd, P.-F. Brevet, R. Antoine, Chiral supramolecular gold-cysteine nanoparticles: Chiroptical and nonlinear optical properties, Prog. Nat. Sci.: Mater. Int. 26 (2016) 455-460.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Emergent properties of an organic semiconductor driven by its molecular chirality
